---
title: "4.5 Back-to-Front and Property Tests"
subtitle: "Implementing Array Algorithms"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit4-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Looping From Back to Front

### Start at the back of the array

You do not have to loop through an array from the front to the back.

You can start at the back of the array and move toward the front during each time through the loop.

The method `getIndexOfLastElementSmallerThanTarget` returns the index of the last element in the array that is smaller than the given argument.

## Back-to-Front Return

### Return stops the loop and method

The `return` statement inside the loop stops the execution of the loop and the method.

It returns the index that is found immediately back to the main method.

The method returns `-1` if there is no number in the array that is smaller than the given number.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: arrayFindSmaller`

What does the following code print out?

Notice that the array and the target are passed in as arguments to the `getIndexOfLastElementSmallerThanTarget` method.

Trace through it keeping track of the array values and the output.

Then add another method that finds the index of the last element greater than the target.

Call this method `getIndexOfLastElementGreaterThanTarget`.

## arrayFindSmaller Starter

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class ArrayFindSmallest
{

    /**
     * @return index of the last number smaller than target
     */
    public static int getIndexOfLastElementSmallerThanTarget(int[] values, int target)
    {
        for (int index = values.length - 1; index >= 0; index--)
        {
            if (values[index] < target)
            {
               return index;
            }
        }
        return -1;
    }

    /**
     * Add a method called getIndexOfLastElementGreaterThanTarget
     *
     * @param int array
     * @param int target
     * @return index of the last number greater than target
     */
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int[] theArray = {-30, -5, 8, 23, 46};
        System.out.println(
                "Last index of element smaller than 50: "
                        + getIndexOfLastElementSmallerThanTarget(theArray, 50));
        System.out.println(
                "Last index of element smaller than 30: "
                        + getIndexOfLastElementSmallerThanTarget(theArray, 30));
        System.out.println(
                "Last index of element smaller than 10: "
                        + getIndexOfLastElementSmallerThanTarget(theArray, 10));
        System.out.println(
                "Last index of element smaller than 0: "
                        + getIndexOfLastElementSmallerThanTarget(theArray, 0));
        System.out.println(
                "Last index of element smaller than -20: "
                        + getIndexOfLastElementSmallerThanTarget(theArray, -20));
        System.out.println(
                "Last index of element smaller than -30: "
                        + getIndexOfLastElementSmallerThanTarget(theArray, -30));
    }
}
```
:::

## arrayFindSmaller Test Targets

### Runestone checks

The first output line should be:

```text
Last index of element smaller than 50: 4
```

The added method should return:

```text
4
```

for this test:

```java
getIndexOfLastElementGreaterThanTarget(
    new int[] {10, 50, 20, 30, 40, 20}, 30)
```

It should return `-1` for target `100`.

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: mcq_array_loop1`

Given the code segment, what will be returned by:

```java
getIndexOfLastElementSmallerThanTarget(values, -13)
```

```java
private int[] values = {-20, -15, 2, 8, 16, 33};

public static int getIndexOfLastElementSmallerThanTarget(int[] values, int compare)
{
    for (int i = values.length - 1; i >= 0; i--)
    {
        if (values[i] < compare)
            return i;
    }
    return -1; // to show none found
}
```

## Answer Reasoning

### Last smaller value from the back

Correct answer: `1`.

Since the method loops from the back toward the front, `-15` is the last value in the array that is less than `-13`, and it is at index `1`.

## Quick Check

### `mchoice:: mcq_array_loop2`

Given the code segment, what will be returned by:

```java
getIndexOfLastElementSmallerThanTarget(values, 7)
```

```java
int[] values = {-20, -15, 2, 8, 16, 33};

public static int getIndexOfLastElementSmallerThanTarget(int[] values, int compare)
{
    for (int i = values.length; i >= 0; i--)
    {
        if (values[i] < compare)
            return i;
    }
    return -1; // to show none found
}
```

## Answer Reasoning

### Start index is out of bounds

Correct answer: you will get an out of bounds error.

You cannot start the index at the length of the array.

You must start at `values.length - 1`.

## Test Property

### At least one, all, or count

Often we loop through an array to see if elements have a particular property, for example all the elements are even or at least one is negative.

On the AP exam, this property is often given as a boolean method to use in the `if` statement to:

::: {.tight-list}
- determine if at least one element has a particular property
- determine if all elements have a particular property
- determine the number of elements having a particular property
:::

## Property Patterns

### Return timing matters

```java
// see if at least one has the property
for (int val : values)
{
    if (isProperty(val))
    {
        return true;
    }
}
return false;
```

```java
// see if all have the property
for (int val : values)
{
    if (!isProperty(val))
    {
        return false;
    }
}
return true;
```

## Count Pattern

### Count the elements with the property

```java
int count = 0;
for (int val : values)
{
    if (isProperty(val))
    {
        count++;
    }
}
```

Determining if **all** have a certain property means that we need to check all elements before returning `true`.

Often we check for the negation of the property to return `false`.

## Mixed-Up Code

### `parsonsprob:: parsons-even-array`

The program segment should count the number of elements in the array that are even using an enhanced for-each loop.

```java
// Block A
int[] arr = {14, -5, 2, 17, 29, -8, 36};
int count = 0;

// Block B
for (int value : arr)
{

// Block C
if (value % 2 == 0)
{

// Block D
count++;

// Block E
} //end conditional

// Block F
} //end for loop
```

## Correct Order

### Count even values

```java
int[] arr = {14, -5, 2, 17, 29, -8, 36};
int count = 0;
for (int value : arr)
{
    if (value % 2 == 0)
    {
        count++;
    } //end conditional
} //end for loop
```

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: exAllOdd`

Write the method `allOdd` to return `true` if all the values in the array are odd.

First write the helper function `isEven` to return `true` or `false` for one value, using `%`.

## exAllOdd Starter

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class Test1
{
    public static boolean isEven(int value)
    {
        // 1. TODO: return true if value is even, false otherwise
        return true;
    }

    public static boolean allOdd(int[] array)
    {
        // 2. TODO: Loop thru the array
        // and return false if any value isEven

        // if all odd, return true
        return true;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int[] a1 = {1, 3, 6};
        int[] a2 = {1, 3, 5};
        System.out.println(allOdd(a1));
        System.out.println(allOdd(a2));
    }
}
```
:::

## exAllOdd Test Targets

### Runestone checks

Expected output from `main`:

```text
false
true
```

Additional tests:

```text
allOdd({10, 20, 30, 40}) -> false
allOdd({37, 41, 55, 99}) -> true
```

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- start back-to-front loops at `values.length - 1`
- use `return` inside a search loop only when the target condition has been found
- return `-1` after a full search when no index is found
- distinguish at-least-one from all-elements property checks
- check the negation of the property to prove an all-elements test false
- use an accumulator count when counting matching elements
:::

## End

### 4.5 Part 2 complete

Next: pairs and duplicates.
